raw_case:
Totally implantable venous access port metastasis in a patient with multiple myeloma
Multiple myeloma is a hematologic malignancy characterized by the proliferation of monoclonal plasma cells within the bone marrow. Extramedullary plasmacytomas, a rare manifestation of multiple myeloma, occur in a small percentage of patients. These plasmacytomas can develop in the skin, often near venous catheter access sites, posing diagnostic challenges due to their rarity. We report a case of a 63-year-old man with multiple myeloma who presented with a mildly painful, swollen left chest port site. A whole-body FDG PET/CT scan revealed a hypermetabolic soft tissue mass surrounding the port site, suggestive of malignancy. Subsequent surgical excision and histopathological analysis confirmed the presence of a metastatic subcutaneous plasmacytoma, consistent with the patient's known multiple myeloma. Cutaneous extramedullary plasmacytomas at central venous catheter sites are rarely reported. Potential mechanisms for tumor development at trauma sites include local expression of chemokines that attract myeloma cells. This case underscores the importance of recognizing cutaneous plasmacytomas in patients with multiple myeloma and venous access ports to avoid misdiagnosis and unnecessary interventions. It contributes to the broader understanding of extramedullary disease manifestations in multiple myeloma and emphasizes the need for thorough investigation and appropriate management in such cases.

Introduction

Multiple myeloma (MM) is a hematologic malignancy characterized by the proliferation of monoclonal plasma cells primarily within the bone marrow. When MM spreads beyond the bone marrow, it is termed extramedullary plasmacytoma. Extramedullary plasmacytomas are a rare manifestation of multiple myeloma, present in about 7% of patients at diagnosis and occurring in an additional 6%-7% over the course of the disease [1]. Cutaneous plasmacytomas typically arise either from direct extension from underlying bone or through hematogenous spread in patients with a high primary disease burden [2]. While sporadic cases of cutaneous plasmacytomas at venous catheter access sites have been documented, they are infrequently encountered by medical oncologists and interventional radiologists [3,4]. This rarity can make identification challenging, and lack of recognition may result in misdiagnosis and unnecessary interventions.We report a rare case of metastatic subcutaneous plasmacytoma that arose after the insertion of a totally implantable venous access port (TIVAP) in a man receiving chemotherapy for multiple myeloma.

Case report

We present the case of a 63-year-old man with multiple myeloma who presented to his outpatient hematology clinic and chemotherapy infusion center with difficulty accessing his TIVAP, which was placed 2 years prior, as well as new onset discomfort at his port site. The patient denied infection symptoms such as fevers and his vital signs were within normal limits. Of note, a FDG PET/CT about 1 year prior did not reveal abnormality at the left chest wall port (Figs. 1A-C). Physical exam noted mild swelling at the superior aspect of the port site, though a chest X-ray confirmed the port's stable position. Laboratory values were only significant for leukopenia of 2.3 × 109/L, which was consistent with the patient's baseline. The patient's hemoglobin, platelets, liver function tests, and basic metabolic panel values were all within normal limits. Initial attempts to draw blood were unsuccessful. An image-guided port check by interventional radiology eventually allowed successful blood return and flushing. During the image guided port check, there was no contrast extravasation from the port or catheter, no venous stenosis, or fibrin sheath. The radiologist recommended resting the port site for 1-2 weeks. On follow-up, due to continued tissue growth, mild tenderness at the port site and continued difficulty accessing the port despite the 2-week rest period, it was decided to replace the TIVAP. A day prior to the scheduled port replacement, our patient obtained his routine scheduled end-of-cycle whole-body FDG PET/CT scan (Figs. 2A-F) which revealed a new hypermetabolic soft tissue mass measuring 4.3 × 1.4 × 3.3 cm (SUV max of 15.8, with an average liver SUV of 2.1) surrounding the left chest port. Findings were concerning for a new metastatic lesion. Additionally, there were findings of multiple new hypermetabolic osseous lesions consistent with disease progression of the multiple myeloma, as partially evaluated in Fig. 2F.One day after the whole-body PET/CT (Fig. 2), the patient presented to the interventional radiology suite for his scheduled TIVAP replacement. After TIVAP placement at a new site, the old left TIVAP was then removed through an elliptical incision at the old insertion scar. White firm tissue was encountered deep to the dermis concerning for tumor. This tissue was excised in fragments to expose the port and its tubing, which were removed in their entirety. The tissue was subsequently sent for surgical pathology in formalin.The pathology of the left chest wall soft tissue mass revealed infiltration by a diffuse sheet of atypical plasma cells exhibiting predominantly immature cytomorphological features such as pleomorphism and prominent one or more small nucleoli (Fig. 3A). These atypical plasma cells were positive for CD138, MUM1 and expressed a kappa restricted pattern of light chain on immunohistochemical stain (Figs. 3B-D). These atypical plasma cells were negative for cytokeratin AE1/AE3, CD20, PAX5, CD3, CD5, lambda and SOX10 immunohistochemical stains. Overall, the histological features of the soft tissue mass biopsy were consistent with monoclonal plasma cells and were compatible with patient's known multiple myeloma history. The patient's treatment management was changed to salvage therapy with elotuzumab, pomalidomide, and dexamethasone due to progression of disease.

Discussion

Cutaneous extramedullary plasmacytomas are rarely reported, especially at sites of trauma such as central venous access points with only a few cases described in the literature. Kerob et al. [3] documented cutaneous lesions in 2 patients who underwent chemotherapy via a central venous catheter, while Rosenblum et al. [4] described a case of multiple subcutaneous plasmacytomas at previous trauma sites, including 2 central venous catheter access locations.Systemic tumor spread is a complex process influenced by local and systemic factors, including direct wound contamination, tumor manipulation, aerosolization, and changes in immune response [5]. Several theories explain plasmacytoma development at trauma sites. A dominant theory suggests that myeloma cells expressing the CXCR-4 chemokine receptor survive and are selected after high-dose induction chemotherapy [5]. The receptor's ligand, stromal cell-derived factor-1 (SDF-1), attracts immune cells and is expressed in areas of trauma and inflammation [5, Curet]. Increased local SDF-1 levels from trauma may attract CXCR-4-positive cells, which, along with inflammatory cytokines, can lead to plasma cell proliferation and focal tumor formation [5].TIVAPs were initially introduced in 1982 [6]. For patients undergoing continuous chemotherapy infusion, a TIVAP is essential for delivering treatment in outpatient settings. Common complications linked with these ports include hemorrhage, pneumothorax, hemothorax, infection, and venous thrombosis [6].Reports of cancer metastasis around TIVAP insertion sites are rare, with only a few cases documented in the literature [[7], [8], [9], [10]]. The literature suggests that head-and-neck, hematologic, and intrathoracic malignancies are the most frequent causes, with squamous cell carcinoma being the most common histologic type. Tumors that are aggressive, progressive, or poorly differentiated are more likely to disseminate to the insertion site compared to those that are localized or well-differentiated.Instances of tumor seeding at the site of a TIVAP in head and neck malignancies have been infrequently reported. Three cases involved patients with squamous cell carcinoma of the tongue, squamous cell carcinoma of the hypopharynx, and spindle cell carcinoma of the hard palate [7,8,11]. It was suggested that the close proximity of the tumor mass to the catheter of the TIVAP, coupled with the tumor's potential resistance to chemotherapy and radiotherapy, could have facilitated the migration and seeding of tumor cells along the catheter and at the port entry site [8].One case involved cancer metastasis around a TIVAP insertion site with advanced pancreatic cancer with suspected metastasis to the para-aortic lymph nodes [10]. Further imaging revealed complete blockage of the left jugular and subclavian veins due to venous thrombosis, affecting the venous angle where the left subclavian and left internal jugular veins meet, known as the “Pirogoff angle.” The venous thrombosis was hypothesized to have impeded lymphatic drainage, leading to reflux. Consequently, lymphatic fluid potentially laden with tumor cells may have accumulated in the subcutaneous cavity used for TIVAP placement [10].Only 2 cases in the literature describe thoracic metastatic lesions developing secondary to central venous catheter placement [12,13]. These cases suggest that catheter insertion in patients with intrathoracic malignant tumors could potentially lead to tumor seeding. Authors emphasize the critical need for stringent sterile precautions to prevent tumor spread, specifically advising that the port incision site remains isolated from surgical fields involved in tumor resection.The TIVAP is a crucial device for patients receiving chemotherapy. It can be implanted using either the Seldinger technique or the cephalic vein cut-down technique [7]. The Seldinger technique, being faster and more effective, is suitable for patients lacking accessible cephalic veins or with small blood vessels [7]. Perioperative complication rates between the 2 techniques show no significant differences [7]. However, it has been hypothesized that the puncture method or technique can be correlated with tumor spread [7]. Previous studies have indicated that the Seldinger technique carries a higher risk of tumor seeding compared to direct cephalic vein cut-down [7]. Additionally, multiple punctures during the procedure may further increase the risk of tumor spread [7].Although cutaneous plasmacytomas generally have favorable 5-year survival rates [14], some cases of cutaneous extramedullary plasmacytoma relapse show a rapid decline in the patients' condition, leading to death [3]. Treatment often involves a combination of chemotherapy, local radiation therapy, and/or surgical resection [14]. However, due to the limited number of case reports on the treatment of cutaneous plasmacytomas, no definitive treatment recommendations exist. Future case reports will provide further insights into this rare disease. This case underscores the need for vigilance and thorough investigation in patients with MM and venous access ports, contributing to a broader understanding of extramedullary disease manifestations and their management. Moreover, metastatic spread at catheter sites is not limited to patients with multiple myeloma; it has been documented in various malignancies. Early recognition and intervention are essential to managing potential complications and improving patient outcomes.

Patient consent

Complete written informed consent was obtained from the patient for the publication of this study and accompanying images.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 63-year-old man with a known diagnosis of multiple myeloma presenting with new localized symptoms.
- **Chief Complaint:** Mildly painful, swollen left chest port site.
- **History of Present Illness:** Patient had difficulty accessing his totally implantable venous access port (TIVAP), placed 2 years prior, along with new discomfort at the port site. He denied fever or other infection-related symptoms. 
- **Past Medical History:** Multiple myeloma.
- **Personal History:** None mentioned.
- **Family History:** None mentioned.
- **Physical Examination:** Mild swelling at the superior aspect of the left chest port site.
- **Ancillary Tests:** 
  - **Past Imaging:** FDG PET/CT performed 1 year prior showed no abnormalities in the port area.
  - **Recent Imaging:** Whole-body FDG PET/CT revealed a new hypermetabolic soft tissue mass (4.3 × 1.4 × 3.3 cm, SUV max 15.8) surrounding the port, concerning for malignancy, along with multiple hypermetabolic osseous lesions indicating disease progression.
  - **Pathology:** Surgical excision of the mass during port replacement revealed a dense infiltration of atypical plasma cells with kappa light chain restriction, confirming a metastatic plasmacytoma.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Infection or Inflammatory Reaction (e.g., port infection or thrombophlebitis):** This was initially considered plausible due to swelling and discomfort but was effectively excluded by a lack of systemic signs (fever, normal vital signs) and no evidence of venous thrombosis or contrast extravasation on imaging.
2. **Mechanical or Structural Complication (e.g., port displacement, venous stenosis):** Physical examination and image-guided port check ruled out mechanical port issues, as the port position and function were noted to be stable initially.
3. **Malignancy (e.g., metastatic lesion, plasmacytoma):** The presence of rapid progression in symptoms, tissue growth, and hypermetabolic activity at the site of the port on PET/CT was highly suggestive of malignancy. Histopathology confirmed the mass as a metastatic plasmacytoma linked to the patient’s known multiple myeloma.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: The patient was diagnosed with metastatic subcutaneous plasmacytoma at the TIVAP site. This was supported by PET/CT findings of a hypermetabolic mass and histological evidence of monoclonal plasma cells consistent with the patient’s history of multiple myeloma.
generate_case.diagnosis_results: Metastatic subcutaneous plasmacytoma at the TIVAP site.

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let's start by looking at this case. The patient is a 63-year-old man with multiple myeloma, which is a plasma cell disorder. His main complaint is a mildly painful and swollen left chest port site. He had a TIVAP placed two years ago, and now he's having trouble accessing it and experiencing new discomfort. No fever or other signs of infection, which is interesting because infections often present with systemic symptoms.

First, I need to consider possible causes of a swollen port site. Common issues include infection (like cellulitis or abscess), thrombosis, mechanical issues with the port, or maybe a hematoma. But he doesn't have fever, so maybe infection is less likely. However, given his history of multiple myeloma, which is a cancer of plasma cells, I should also think about possible malignant involvement. Myeloma can lead to plasmacytomas, which are tumors formed by malignant plasma cells. These can be either solitary or part of disease progression.

Looking at the imaging results. The past PET/CT a year ago was normal around the port. Now, the recent PET/CT shows a new hypermetabolic mass around the port with high SUV max (15.8), which is concerning for malignancy. High SUV usually indicates high metabolic activity, often seen in cancers. Additionally, there are multiple new bone lesions, which suggests his myeloma is progressing. The pathology from the excised mass confirmed atypical plasma cells with kappa restriction. Since myeloma cells are monoclonal plasma cells, and light chain restriction (like kappa here) is a hallmark of malignancy in this context, this makes sense.

So, the localized mass at the port site isn't just a local infection or inflammation. The presence of plasma cells with kappa restriction points towards a plasmacytoma. Plasmacytomas can be either primary (solitary) or secondary to multiple myeloma. In this case, since the patient already has a diagnosis of multiple myeloma and there's evidence of disease progression (new osseous lesions), this is likely a metastatic plasmacytoma from his myeloma. The port site might be a site of extramedullary disease spread. Extramedullary plasmacytomas can occur, and in the context of known myeloma, this would be a sign of disease progression or metastasis.

Putting it all together: The patient has multiple myeloma with new progression evidenced by bone lesions and a new soft tissue mass. The pathology confirms that the mass is a plasmacytoma. Therefore, the final diagnosis is a metastatic plasmacytoma at the port site as part of his multiple myeloma progression.
results.content:
```
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient has a history of multiple myeloma, a plasma cell malignancy, and presents with localized symptoms at his TIVAP site. The absence of fever or systemic infection symptoms reduces the likelihood of a primary infectious etiology (e.g., cellulitis, abscess).  
<step 2> The new hypermetabolic soft tissue mass around the port on PET/CT (SUV max 15.8) strongly suggests malignancy. The high metabolic activity and structural involvement align with aggressive pathology, such as neoplastic infiltration.  
<step 3> The concurrent multiple hypermetabolic osseous lesions indicate systemic progression of multiple myeloma, supporting the context of advanced disease.  
<step 4> Pathologic confirmation of atypical plasma cells with kappa light chain restriction confirms clonal plasma cell proliferation, consistent with plasmacytoma. In the setting of known multiple myeloma, this represents a metastatic/extramedullary manifestation rather than a primary solitary plasmacytoma.  
<step 5> The temporal association with port placement (2 years prior) suggests possible seeding or local spread of myeloma cells to the site, a recognized but uncommon complication of invasive devices in hematologic malignancies.  

### Answer:
Metastatic plasmacytoma (extramedullary disease progression of multiple myeloma) at the TIVAP site.

reasoning_eval.step: The patient has a history of multiple myeloma, a plasma cell malignancy, and presents with localized symptoms at his TIVAP site. The absence of fever or systemic infection symptoms reduces the likelihood of a primary infectious etiology (e.g., cellulitis, abscess).

The new hypermetabolic soft tissue mass around the port on PET/CT (SUV max 15.8) strongly suggests malignancy. The high metabolic activity and structural involvement align with aggressive pathology, such as neoplastic infiltration.
The concurrent multiple hypermetabolic osseous lesions indicate systemic progression of multiple myeloma, supporting the context of advanced disease.

Pathologic confirmation of atypical plasma cells with kappa light chain restriction confirms clonal plasma cell proliferation, consistent with plasmacytoma. In the setting of known multiple myeloma, this represents a metastatic/extramedullary manifestation rather than a primary solitary plasmacytoma.

The temporal association with port placement (2 years prior) suggests possible seeding or local spread of myeloma cells to the site, a recognized but uncommon complication of invasive devices in hematologic malignancies.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: Step 1: Initial consideration of infection or inflammatory reaction (e.g., port infection or thrombophlebitis) was plausible due to swelling and discomfort but was excluded by the absence of systemic signs (fever, normal vital signs) and no evidence of venous thrombosis or contrast extravasation on imaging.

Step 2: Mechanical or structural complications (e.g., port displacement, venous stenosis) were ruled out through physical examination and image-guided port check, which confirmed stable port position and function.
Step 3: Malignancy (e.g., metastatic lesion, plasmacytoma) was highly suggested by rapid symptom progression, tissue growth, and hypermetabolic activity at the port site on PET/CT.
Step 4: Histopathology confirmed the mass as a metastatic plasmacytoma linked to the patient's known multiple myeloma.

Step 5: The final diagnosis was metastatic subcutaneous plasmacytoma at the TIVAP site, supported by PET/CT findings of a hypermetabolic mass and histological evidence of monoclonal plasma cells consistent with the patient’s history of multiple myeloma.
